IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—learning multilingual embeddings for many (59) languages using only monolingual data plus bilingual dictionaries—which is valuable for low-resource scenarios and could change practitioner choices away from heavy parallel/corpus-based methods. Its introduction of multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA (which better correlate with downstream tasks) and the open web portal/code for standardized intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation substantially improves reproducibility and benchmarking in the area. These contributions are likely to be widely used and to open follow-on work on dictionary-based multilingual representations and improved intrinsic evaluation, though they are not clearly transformative across all of NLP. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—scalable multilingual embeddings for many languages using only monolingual data plus bilingual dictionaries—and demonstrates methods (multiCluster, multiCCA) applied to 59 languages, which is likely to influence how researchers approach low-resource multilingual representation learning. The introduction of multiQVEC-CCA and evidence that it correlates better with downstream tasks improves intrinsic evaluation practice and, together with the web portal and open-source code, makes the results broadly usable and reproducible. While these contributions are strong and broadly applicable, they extend and combine existing ideas rather than overturning the field, so the likely impact is substantial but not fully transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper proposes two practical multilingual embedding methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) and principled intrinsic metrics (multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA) while requiring only monolingual data plus bilingual dictionaries, which is squarely within the core interests of top-tier NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on a large multilingual set (59 languages), reports correlations with downstream tasks (document classification, dependency parsing), compares to relevant baselines, and releases code and a web evaluation portal — matching the methodological and reproducibility standards expected. Overall the topic, audience, formulation, and evaluation align well with the research community for a top venue. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic (multilingual word embeddings) and proposes two practical methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) along with evaluation innovations (multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA). The work evaluates on a large multilingual scale (59 languages) and measures correlation with downstream tasks (RCV document classification, Universal Dependencies parsing), which matches the methodological and empirical standards expected at top NLP/ML venues. The release of a web portal and code further supports reproducibility and community relevance, and the problem framing and baselines are appropriate for the intended audience.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes two practical multilingual embedding methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) and principled intrinsic metrics (multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA) while requiring only monolingual data plus bilingual dictionaries, which is squarely within the core interests of top-tier NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on a large multilingual set (59 languages), reports correlations with downstream tasks (document classification, dependency parsing), compares to relevant baselines, and releases code and a web evaluation portal — matching the methodological and reproducibility standards expected. Overall the topic, audience, formulation, and evaluation align well with the research community for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning topic (multilingual word embeddings) and proposes two practical methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) along with evaluation innovations (multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA). The work evaluates on a large multilingual scale (59 languages) and measures correlation with downstream tasks (RCV document classification, Universal Dependencies parsing), which matches the methodological and empirical standards expected at top NLP/ML venues. The release of a web portal and code further supports reproducibility and community relevance, and the problem framing and baselines are appropriate for the intended audience.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: two dictionary-based methods (multiCluster, multiCCA) are evaluated across 59 languages and multiple downstream tasks, and multiQVEC(-CCA) are shown to correlate better with extrinsic tasks. The proposed mathematical remedies (QVEC-CCA) address stated theoretical issues with QVEC and the CCA-based mapping approach is standard and sound. However, the experimental methodology has minor weaknesses: hyperparameters are not reported, some baselines (e.g., multiSkip) are trained under different data regimes, and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which weakens some comparisons. Overall the results justify the conclusions, but these reporting gaps prevent a full technical clean bill of health. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments across 59 languages, comparisons to relevant baselines (multiSkip, translation-invariance MF), and correlation analyses with downstream tasks showing multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA outperform prior intrinsic metrics. The proposed methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) and the QVEC-CCA adaptation address the stated theoretical issues and are plausibly sound given the summary. However, the experimental methodology has minor weaknesses: hyperparameters are not reported and some baseline training regimes differ (e.g., multiSkip trained only on parallel data), and no mention of statistical significance testing is given, which limits reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims. Overall the technical work is solid with only modest methodological shortcomings that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: two dictionary-based methods (multiCluster, multiCCA) are evaluated across 59 languages and multiple downstream tasks, and multiQVEC(-CCA) are shown to correlate better with extrinsic tasks. The proposed mathematical remedies (QVEC-CCA) address stated theoretical issues with QVEC and the CCA-based mapping approach is standard and sound. However, the experimental methodology has minor weaknesses: hyperparameters are not reported, some baselines (e.g., multiSkip) are trained under different data regimes, and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which weakens some comparisons. Overall the results justify the conclusions, but these reporting gaps prevent a full technical clean bill of health.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments across 59 languages, comparisons to relevant baselines (multiSkip, translation-invariance MF), and correlation analyses with downstream tasks showing multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA outperform prior intrinsic metrics. The proposed methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) and the QVEC-CCA adaptation address the stated theoretical issues and are plausibly sound given the summary. However, the experimental methodology has minor weaknesses: hyperparameters are not reported and some baseline training regimes differ (e.g., multiSkip trained only on parallel data), and no mention of statistical significance testing is given, which limits reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims. Overall the technical work is solid with only modest methodological shortcomings that do not overturn the main conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents substantial experimentation—training embeddings for 59 languages, evaluating with multiple intrinsic (multiQVEC, multiQVEC-CCA, word similarity) and extrinsic (document classification, dependency parsing) metrics, and comparing to reasonable baselines—supporting the main claims. It also provides code and an evaluation portal and discusses specific limitations (QVEC theoretical issues, training cost for translation-invariance MF, and multiSkip’s parallel-corpus reliance). However, ablation/sensitivity analyses are not reported and hyperparameters are not provided, which weakens reproducibility and understanding of robustness. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, so overall this is strong work with a few important experimental/reporting gaps. | B(4): Experiments are broadly comprehensive: evaluation covers 59 languages, compares to relevant baselines (multiSkip, translation-invariance MF), uses multiple intrinsic metrics (multiQVEC, multiQVEC-CCA, word similarity, word translation) and extrinsic tasks (RCV document classification, dependency parsing) to validate claims. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not reported (no hyperparameters reported and no explicit ablations), which is a notable gap. Related work coverage is stated as adequate and some limitations/failure cases are discussed (QVEC weaknesses, training difficulty for translation-invariance MF, multiSkip data limitations). Reproducibility is partially supported by released code and a web portal but undermined by missing hyperparameter details.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and follows a clear flow from methods (multiCluster, multiCCA) to experiments and conclusions, with main contributions stated early (new methods, multiQVEC(-CCA), and an evaluation portal). Notation and theoretical points are clearly motivated (e.g., QVEC’s shortcomings and the move to QVEC-CCA), and related work coverage is adequate. Writing quality is reported as good, but a presentation omission (hyperparameters not reported) and limited detail about figures/tables in the summary suggest minor clarity/presentation gaps. Overall, the paper is well-written with only small issues to address. | B(4): The summary indicates the paper is well-organized (clearly separated methodology—multiCluster and multiCCA—experiments on 59 languages, and conclusions) and states its main contributions early (new methods, multiQVEC-CCA, and an evaluation portal). Notation and methodological descriptions appear coherent (CCA-based projections, cluster-ID training) and related work coverage is adequate, while writing quality is explicitly noted as good. A notable presentation omission is that hyperparameters are not reported, which is a minor clarity/reproducibility issue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes clearly differentiated, practical methods (multiCluster that groups translationally equivalent words into multilingual cluster IDs, and multiCCA that uses CCA to map monolingual embeddings into an English space) that require only monolingual data plus bilingual dictionaries and scale to 59 languages. It also addresses known evaluation issues by adapting QVEC to multiQVEC and introducing multiQVEC-CCA to fix QVEC’s theoretical shortcomings, and provides a reproducible evaluation portal. These represent notable, non-trivial extensions and engineering-scale contributions compared to baselines like multiSkip and translation-invariance factorization, but do not claim a radical new theoretical paradigm, so the novelty is substantial but not groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper presents notable novelty by proposing two dictionary-based estimation methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) that enable training multilingual embeddings for 59 languages using only monolingual corpora plus pairwise bilingual dictionaries — a clear new practical perspective compared to parallel-corpus methods like multiSkip and the translation-invariance MF baseline. The technical ideas are non-trivial adaptations (multiCluster groups translationally equivalent words and trains cluster-ID embeddings; multiCCA uses CCA to project monolingual spaces into an English pivot space) and are clearly differentiated from prior work. Its evaluation contribution (multiQVEC-CCA) directly addresses stated theoretical shortcomings of QVEC and provides a more principled intrinsic metric, making the overall contribution informative to experts even if not revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes clearly differentiated, practical methods (multiCluster that groups translationally equivalent words into multilingual cluster IDs, and multiCCA that uses CCA to map monolingual embeddings into an English space) that require only monolingual data plus bilingual dictionaries and scale to 59 languages. It also addresses known evaluation issues by adapting QVEC to multiQVEC and introducing multiQVEC-CCA to fix QVEC’s theoretical shortcomings, and provides a reproducible evaluation portal. These represent notable, non-trivial extensions and engineering-scale contributions compared to baselines like multiSkip and translation-invariance factorization, but do not claim a radical new theoretical paradigm, so the novelty is substantial but not groundbreaking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents notable novelty by proposing two dictionary-based estimation methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) that enable training multilingual embeddings for 59 languages using only monolingual corpora plus pairwise bilingual dictionaries — a clear new practical perspective compared to parallel-corpus methods like multiSkip and the translation-invariance MF baseline. The technical ideas are non-trivial adaptations (multiCluster groups translationally equivalent words and trains cluster-ID embeddings; multiCCA uses CCA to project monolingual spaces into an English pivot space) and are clearly differentiated from prior work. Its evaluation contribution (multiQVEC-CCA) directly addresses stated theoretical shortcomings of QVEC and provides a more principled intrinsic metric, making the overall contribution informative to experts even if not revolutionary.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides code and uses publicly known datasets (RCV, UD v1, MWS353, MEN, Rare Words), and the two methods (multiCluster, multiCCA) and evaluation metrics (multiQVEC, multiQVEC-CCA, word translation, downstream tasks) are described, so a reimplementation is feasible. However, hyperparameters and detailed training/configuration choices are not reported (summary: "Hyperparameters reported: False"), and the preprocessing pipeline is not clearly specified in the extracted facts. Evaluation metrics are introduced, but the summary does not confirm that all procedural details (e.g., exact correlations, splits, training epochs, window sizes) are precisely defined, so some important reproduction details are missing. | B(3): The paper names datasets and evaluation metrics and the code is available, which helps reproduction. However, hyperparameters and training configurations are explicitly not reported and the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described, while the sourcing/assembly details for the 59-language training corpora are not specified in the summary. Evaluation metrics are introduced (multiQVEC, multiQVEC-CCA, word translation via cosine NN) but with missing implementation/training detail, so reproduction is only partial without the missing experimental specifics.

However, hyperparameters and detailed training/configuration choices are not reported (summary: "Hyperparameters reported: False"), and the preprocessing pipeline is not clearly specified in the extracted facts. Evaluation metrics are introduced, but the summary does not confirm that all procedural details (e.g., exact correlations, splits, training epochs, window sizes) are precisely defined, so some important reproduction details are missing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to appropriate recent baselines (multiSkip variants and Gardner et al.'s translation-invariance matrix factorization) and evaluates across multiple intrinsic and extrinsic tasks, including newly proposed multiQVEC-CCA. Comparisons appear largely fair and analyzed (differences between multiCluster/multiCCA and baselines are explained), but there are some caveats noted in the paper summary—e.g., multiSkip is trained only on parallel corpora while other methods use monolingual data, and training translation-invariance models at larger context sizes is computationally challenging—that suggest minor fairness/coverage limitations. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations of prior metrics/methods are acknowledged. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (multiSkip variants and the translation-invariance matrix factorization of Gardner et al., 2015), evaluates across a large set of 59 languages and multiple downstream tasks, and reforms/compares intrinsic metrics (QVEC, QVEC-CCA) showing improved correlation. It cites and discusses relevant competing approaches and acknowledges limitations and when prior methods have comparable performance. Some fairness issues remain (e.g., multiSkip trained only on parallel corpora while other methods use monolingual data, and the translation-invariance MF was constrained by computational limits), which are noted in the paper and constitute minor omissions rather than major flaws.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to appropriate recent baselines (multiSkip variants and Gardner et al.'s translation-invariance matrix factorization) and evaluates across multiple intrinsic and extrinsic tasks, including newly proposed multiQVEC-CCA. Comparisons appear largely fair and analyzed (differences between multiCluster/multiCCA and baselines are explained), but there are some caveats noted in the paper summary—e.g., multiSkip is trained only on parallel corpora while other methods use monolingual data, and training translation-invariance models at larger context sizes is computationally challenging—that suggest minor fairness/coverage limitations. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations of prior metrics/methods are acknowledged.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (multiSkip variants and the translation-invariance matrix factorization of Gardner et al., 2015), evaluates across a large set of 59 languages and multiple downstream tasks, and reforms/compares intrinsic metrics (QVEC, QVEC-CCA) showing improved correlation. It cites and discusses relevant competing approaches and acknowledges limitations and when prior methods have comparable performance. Some fairness issues remain (e.g., multiSkip trained only on parallel corpora while other methods use monolingual data, and the translation-invariance MF was constrained by computational limits), which are noted in the paper and constitute minor omissions rather than major flaws.

main_claims: Propose two dictionary-based methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) for estimating multilingual word embeddings that require only monolingual data and pairwise bilingual dictionaries and use them to train embeddings for 59 languages.
Adapt QVEC to multilingual evaluation (multiQVEC) and introduce multiQVEC-CCA, addressing theoretical shortcomings of QVEC and showing better correlation with downstream tasks.
Provide a web portal (and open-source releases) for evaluating multilingual embeddings with a suite of intrinsic and extrinsic metrics to facilitate reproducible research.
Show that multiQVEC and multiQVEC-CCA achieve better correlations with extrinsic tasks compared to previously used intrinsic metrics.
_summary.methodology: They propose multiCluster, which groups translationally equivalent words into multilingual clusters using bilingual dictionaries and trains embeddings for cluster IDs (using skipgram) on concatenated modified monolingual corpora; and multiCCA, which trains monolingual embeddings and uses CCA-based linear projections to map other languages into the English embedding space (using the English space as the multilingual space).
datasets: Universal Dependencies v1 (subset) for dependency parsing
en-it MWS353 (cross-lingual word similarity; split into dev/test)
English/Danish/Italian supersense tag annotations (linguistic resource for QVEC variants)
baselines: multiSkip (variant of Coulmance et al., 2015 and Guo et al., 2016)
metrics: monolingual and cross-lingual word similarity (e.g., WordSim-353, MEN, MWS353)
correlation with downstream tasks (document classification/text categorization and dependency parsing)
limitations: QVEC is not invariant to linear transformations of the embedding basis and its cumulative-sum scoring is unnormalized (favoring higher dimensionality) — motivating QVEC-CCA.
Intrinsic evaluation metrics are not perfect and must be assessed by correlation with downstream tasks.
Training the translation-invariance matrix factorization embeddings with larger context window sizes is computationally challenging.
multiSkip is trained only on parallel corpora while some other methods also use monolingual corpora.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multilingual word embeddings / representation learning
